detailedSummary:
The candidate's performance was significantly compromised by a clear lack of independent problem-solving and integrity during the technical assessment. The screen was shared continuously throughout the recording. However, multiple instances were observed where the candidate navigated away from the interview platform to an external browser tab hosting ChatGPT.

At approximately 1:09, the candidate explicitly switched to ChatGPT to search for a 'Reverse String' Java solution. They then copied the entire Java code block provided by ChatGPT (around 1:20) and pasted it into the coding editor (around 1:23). When running this code, it failed initial test cases due to issues in how the output was printed or perceived by the platform. Instead of attempting to debug or understand the core issue independently, the candidate reverted to ChatGPT around 2:32, requesting a solution that would pass all test cases. A second, more extensive code block from ChatGPT was then copied (around 2:56) and pasted (around 3:00), overwriting their previous attempt. Even with this second copied solution, the candidate continued to struggle with basic integration, making errors in passing the correct string variables to the `reverseString` method for each test case, and in correctly formatting the output for the platform. This resulted in continuous test failures up until the end of the recording.

The core algorithm (two-pointer approach for string reversal) is indeed optimal with O(N) time and O(1) space complexity, but the candidate did not demonstrate any original thought or implementation. The complete reliance on an external AI tool for the solution, coupled with an inability to effectively integrate and debug the provided code within the assessment environment, reflects a critical lack of fundamental coding and problem-solving skills expected for a Software Engineer I role. Furthermore, the provided 'Final Submitted Code' being empty, despite code being present and run in the video, points to either a technical issue on their part or a failure to properly submit their work, which is also a negative indicator. The presence of a persistent 'shhhh' sound also indicates a potentially distracting environment.
